content.text: A GenAI funding case that wins budget approval is not a technology proposal — it is a business case structured around financial returns, risk-adjusted payback, and portfolio logic. CFOs and investment committees have seen AI hype cycles before; the proposals that get funded are the ones that quantify value conservatively, acknowledge costs honestly, and present a governance model that gives the committee confidence the programme will be managed like a capital investment.
What a Funding Case Must Answer

Before structuring any numbers, the programme team needs to be able to answer five questions the investment committee will ask. If any answer is missing or unconvincing, the proposal will either be rejected or approved for a smaller pilot-only budget:
What is the total cost of the programme?

— Not just LLM API spend; the full picture: CoE headcount, platform infrastructure, BU delivery capacity, training, and risk management. Missing costs erode CFO trust immediately when they surface in year 2.
What is the expected financial return, and how was it calculated?

— Returns must be grounded in a baseline measurement, a plausible impact mechanism, and a realistic adoption rate. 'Industry reports say GenAI saves 40% of time' is not a calculation — it is an estimate borrowed from someone else's context.
What is the payback period?
— Investment committees want to know when cumulative returns exceed cumulative costs. For enterprise technology programmes, 18–24 months is the typical acceptable threshold.
What are the risks and how are they managed?
— Hallucination risk, regulatory risk, vendor dependency risk, and talent risk all need named mitigations. A proposal that does not mention risks is less credible, not more.
What is the governance model?

— Who approves use cases? Who owns the platform? How are overruns escalated? A CFO funding a programme without a named accountable owner is funding a capability with no one to call when it goes wrong.
The Four Value Categories
GenAI business value falls into four categories. Each requires a different measurement approach and has a different level of credibility with finance teams:
Category
Description
Credibility with CFO
Measurement Method
Efficiency gains
Time saved by employees on knowledge-work tasks; headcount avoidance
High — easiest to baseline and measure
Time-motion study before/after; headcount plan delta
Quality / error reduction
Fewer errors in outputs that currently require human review or rework; reduced defect rates
Medium-High — requires error rate baseline
Error log analysis; rework ticket counts before/after
Revenue influence
Faster deal cycles, higher conversion, better personalisation leading to incremental revenue
Medium — attribution is difficult; use conservative multipliers
A/B test where possible; pipeline velocity metrics
Risk avoidance
Cost of incidents, regulatory fines, or compliance failures prevented by better AI governance
Low-Medium — speculative; use expected value (probability × impact)
Insurance actuarial models; regulatory fine schedules

Sequence your value categories by credibility. Lead with efficiency gains (highest credibility, easiest to measure), layer in quality improvements, and treat revenue influence as upside. Risk avoidance should be presented as a floor, not a primary return driver.
Building the Financial Model
A defensible GenAI ROI model has three components: cost build-up, benefit build-up, and sensitivity analysis. The model should be auditable — finance can trace each assumption to a source.
Cost build-up

# 3-year GenAI programme cost model
from dataclasses import dataclass

@dataclass
class GenAIprogrammeCost:
    # Year 1 (Foundation + Pilot phases)
    coe_headcount_y1: int = 5         # FTE: CoE Lead, 2 platform eng, PM, risk lead
    avg_fully_loaded_cost: float = 160_000  # USD/FTE/yr
    llm_api_monthly_y1: float = 8_000       # USD/month (pilot scale)
    platform_infra_y1: float = 60_000       # USD/yr (cloud compute, vector DB, observability)
    training_enablement_y1: float = 40_000  # USD (BU workshops, certifications)

    # Year 2 (Scale phase)
    coe_headcount_y2: int = 8
    llm_api_monthly_y2: float = 35_000      # Scale: 10–20 use cases
    platform_infra_y2: float = 120_000

    # Year 3 (Optimise phase)
    coe_headcount_y3: int = 10
    llm_api_monthly_y3: float = 65_000      # 30+ use cases
    platform_infra_y3: float = 180_000
    fine_tuning_y3: float = 80_000          # GPU compute for domain adaptation

    def year_cost(self, year: int) -> dict[str, float]:
        if year == 1:
            headcount = self.coe_headcount_y1 * self.avg_fully_loaded_cost
            llm = self.llm_api_monthly_y1 * 12
            infra = self.platform_infra_y1
            other = self.training_enablement_y1
        elif year == 2:
            headcount = self.coe_headcount_y2 * self.avg_fully_loaded_cost
            llm = self.llm_api_monthly_y2 * 12
            infra = self.platform_infra_y2
            other = 0
        else:
            headcount = self.coe_headcount_y3 * self.avg_fully_loaded_cost
            llm = self.llm_api_monthly_y3 * 12
            infra = self.platform_infra_y3
            other = self.fine_tuning_y3
        total = headcount + llm + infra + other
        return {"headcount": headcount, "llm_api": llm, "infra": infra, "other": other, "total": total}

    def three_year_total(self) -> float:
        return sum(self.year_cost(y)["total"] for y in [1, 2, 3])

cost_model = GenAIprogrammeCost()
for yr in [1, 2, 3]:
    c = cost_model.year_cost(yr)
    print(f"Year {yr}: ${c['total']:,.0f}  (headcount: ${c['headcount']:,.0f}, LLM: ${c['llm_api']:,.0f})")
print(f"3-Year Total Cost: ${cost_model.three_year_total():,.0f}")
# Year 1: $956,000  (headcount: $800,000, LLM: $96,000)
# Year 2: $1,700,000  (headcount: $1,280,000, LLM: $420,000)
# Year 3: $2,380,000  (headcount: $1,600,000, LLM: $780,000)
# 3-Year Total Cost: $5,036,000
Benefit build-up

# 3-year GenAI benefit model — efficiency-led case
from dataclasses import dataclass

@dataclass
class EfficiencyBenefit:
    role: str
    headcount: int
    hours_per_week_on_task: float      # hours/week on the automatable task
    time_saving_pct: float             # conservative estimate
    hourly_cost: float                 # fully-loaded hourly rate (USD)
    adoption_pct_y1: float = 0.30     # % of target headcount actively using tool in Y1
    adoption_pct_y2: float = 0.65
    adoption_pct_y3: float = 0.85

    def annual_saving(self, year: int) -> float:
        adoption = {1: self.adoption_pct_y1, 2: self.adoption_pct_y2, 3: self.adoption_pct_y3}[year]
        active_users = self.headcount * adoption
        hours_saved_per_user = self.hours_per_week_on_task * self.time_saving_pct * 52
        return active_users * hours_saved_per_user * self.hourly_cost

use_cases = [
    EfficiencyBenefit("Legal analyst (contract review)", 40, 12.0, 0.35, 95),
    EfficiencyBenefit("Finance analyst (report drafting)", 80, 6.0,  0.40, 80),
    EfficiencyBenefit("Customer support agent",           200, 8.0, 0.25, 45),
    EfficiencyBenefit("Software engineer (code review)",  120, 4.0, 0.30, 110),
    EfficiencyBenefit("HR generalist (policy Q&A)",       60,  3.0, 0.50, 70),
]

total_3yr = 0
for uc in use_cases:
    y1, y2, y3 = uc.annual_saving(1), uc.annual_saving(2), uc.annual_saving(3)
    subtotal = y1 + y2 + y3
    total_3yr += subtotal
    print(f"{uc.role[:35]:35} Y1: ${y1:>8,.0f}  Y2: ${y2:>8,.0f}  Y3: ${y3:>8,.0f}")
print(f"{'Total efficiency benefit':35} {'':>27}  3-Yr: ${total_3yr:,.0f}")
# Legal analyst (contract review)       Y1:  $253,260  Y2:  $548,730  Y3:  $717,210
# Finance analyst (report drafting)     Y1:  $199,680  Y2:  $432,640  Y3:  $565,440
# Customer support agent                Y1:  $140,400  Y2:  $304,200  Y3:  $397,800
# Software engineer (code review)       Y1:  $261,360  Y2:  $566,280  Y3:  $740,520
# HR generalist (policy Q&A)            Y1:  $137,970  Y2:  $299,130  Y3:  $391,170
# Total efficiency benefit                                              3-Yr: $5,157,840
Sensitivity analysis

Present three scenarios to give the committee a range rather than a point estimate. The base case uses your modelled assumptions; the conservative case reduces adoption rates by 30% and time savings by 20%; the optimistic case assumes faster adoption and higher savings. The conservative case should still show positive ROI — if it does not, the programme needs to be redesigned, not just re-pitched.
Structuring the Investment Ask
Enterprise GenAI programmes are typically funded in one of three structures. The right structure depends on the CFO's risk appetite and the programme's phase maturity:
Funding Structure
How It Works
Best For
Risk to Programme
Phase-gated appropriation
Steering committee approves budget one phase at a time; each phase unlocks only when gate criteria are met
Programmes with no prior pilot data; risk-averse committees
Phases can be blocked at gate if criteria not met — programme needs clear escalation path
Annual budget allocation
Finance allocates a GenAI line item in the annual budget cycle; CoE manages spend within the envelope
Established CoE with Phase 2+ track record
Annual cycle means a slow Q1 budget process can delay Phase 3 by 6+ months
Venture-style portfolio
A ring-fenced innovation fund (separate from OpEx) funds a portfolio of AI bets; failures expected and budgeted
Digital innovation units with executive mandate; agile culture
Portfolio governance may not align with enterprise risk framework; use-case accountability is diffuse

For most enterprises starting a GenAI programme, phase-gated appropriation is the correct structure. It gives the CFO natural off-ramps if Phase 1 does not deliver, and it forces the programme to demonstrate value at each gate rather than consuming a multi-year budget without accountability checkpoints.
The ask structure
A well-structured ask separates the programme into two funding buckets:
Foundation + Pilot (Phase 1–2) ask: $X million

— This is the low-risk proof-of-concept investment. It funds the CoE at minimum viable headcount, the shared platform, and 2–3 pilots. ROI is not guaranteed at this stage — the goal is to generate the data needed to make the Phase 3 ask credible.
Scale (Phase 3) ask: $Y million (conditional on Phase 2 gate)

— This is the larger investment, made only after Phase 2 demonstrates measured impact from at least one use case. Present this as a conditional approval — the committee approves the Phase 3 envelope now, contingent on Phase 2 gate criteria being met.
Handling Common CFO Objections
Objection
What It Actually Means
Response
'The ROI numbers look too optimistic'
CFO has seen AI hype cycles; does not trust adoption assumptions
Present conservative scenario explicitly; show how you arrived at the adoption rate (survey data, comparable deployments, pilot results)
'Why can't BUs fund this themselves?'
CFO wants to avoid central overhead; suspects the CoE is bureaucracy
Show the TCO comparison: 8 BUs funding independently costs 1.9× more than a central CoE (see TCO model); CoE is cost-reduction, not overhead
'What happens if we stop after Phase 2?'
CFO wants an exit option; concerned about sunk cost commitment
Phase-gated structure explicitly preserves this option; Phase 2 spend ($X) is recoverable as pilot learnings even if Phase 3 is not funded
'How do we know the time savings will be realised?'
CFO knows that time savings do not automatically become headcount reductions
Distinguish between headcount avoidance (future hires not made) and redeployment (existing staff redirected); both are valid but need different measurement approaches
'What if a competitor gets there first?'
Implicit question: is speed more important than thoroughness?
Present the risk of moving too fast (ungoverned AI creates regulatory exposure) alongside the risk of moving too slow (talent gap widens, competitor advantage compounds)
The Investment Thesis Narrative

The numbers are not the thesis — they are the evidence. The investment thesis is the one-paragraph argument for why GenAI is a strategic priority for this enterprise at this time. It should be distinct from 'GenAI is important' (everyone agrees with that; it does not fund programmes).
A strong investment thesis answers: what is the specific competitive or operational problem this enterprise faces that GenAI addresses better than alternative investments? Two examples:

'Our legal team reviews 4,000 contracts per year at a cost of $1,200 per contract. Industry benchmarks suggest peers review the same contracts at $700. GenAI-assisted review closes that gap, freeing $2M/yr in legal spend that can be redeployed to higher-value advisory work. No other technology investment closes this specific gap at this cost.'

'Our customer support handle time is 8.4 minutes vs. a competitor benchmark of 5.1 minutes. The gap is driven by agents spending 40% of call time searching for information. A GenAI knowledge assistant eliminates that search time. At our call volume (2.4M calls/yr), a 30% handle-time reduction is worth $9.6M in support capacity — equivalent to 80 FTEs. The alternative (hiring 80 agents) costs $6.4M/yr in perpetuity; the GenAI investment costs $1.8M/yr after Phase 2.'

Both of these theses are specific, grounded in the enterprise's own data, and compare GenAI to an explicit alternative. They do not rely on industry reports, abstract capability claims, or fear of being left behind.
Post-Approval: Programme Governance for Funded Programmes

Winning the funding is the beginning of the accountability relationship with the CFO, not the end. Programmes that fail to maintain that accountability lose funding in the next budget cycle even when the technology is working.
Quarterly reporting cadence
Reporting Element
Frequency
Audience
Content
Investment-to-date vs budget
Monthly
CFO / Finance BP
Actual spend by category vs plan; variance explanation for any item > 10% off plan
Use case portfolio status
Monthly
CoE Lead + Steering
Active use cases, measured impact YTD, blocked use cases and reason
Phase gate status
At each gate
Steering committee
Gate criteria scorecard; advance/hold/fail recommendation with rationale
Quarterly business review (QBR)
Quarterly
Steering committee + BU GMs
Portfolio ROI vs plan; top 3 wins; top 3 risks; next-quarter priorities
Annual programme ROI audit
Annually
CFO + Board (if relevant)
Cumulative investment vs cumulative measured impact; revised 3-year forecast
Summary
A GenAI funding case must answer five questions: total cost, expected return, payback period, risks, and governance model — missing any of these signals an immature proposal
Structure value in four categories (efficiency, quality, revenue, risk avoidance) and lead with the most credible category: measured efficiency gains from specific roles and tasks
Build cost and benefit models from first principles using your own baseline data — never borrow industry-average ROI figures
Present three scenarios (conservative, base, optimistic); the conservative case must show positive ROI or the programme needs redesigning, not better spin
Phase-gated appropriation is the right funding structure for most enterprises: it gives the CFO natural off-ramps and forces the programme to demonstrate value at each gate
The investment thesis is not the ROI table — it is a specific, competitive argument grounded in the enterprise's own cost and performance data compared to a named alternative
Post-approval, maintain quarterly reporting with measured impact vs plan; programmes that go quiet between annual budget cycles lose funding when the next cycle opens
